question: Based on this radiograph, which is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image for overall anatomy and the status of both hips. This is an AP pelvis radiograph showing a native right hip and a left femoral prosthesis (hemiarthroplasty) with a femoral stem and spherical prosthetic head.

Next, I relate what I see to the question, which asks for the most likely diagnosis. On the left, the prosthetic femoral head is not seated within the acetabular fossa — it is displaced superolaterally, lying outside the acetabular margin rather than articulating concentrically with the socket. The femoral stem remains within the femoral canal and the prosthetic components appear intact.

Then, I consider how each option lines up with those observations. A dislocation of the left bipolar hemiarthroplasty fits the clear malposition of the prosthetic head outside the acetabulum. A periprosthetic femoral shaft fracture would show cortical discontinuity or a clear fracture line of the femur, which is not seen. Aseptic loosening of the femoral component would typically show component subsidence or lucent lines around the stem; the stem here appears well seated without obvious migration. Advanced left hip osteoarthritis is not applicable because the joint is replaced and the radiograph demonstrates a displaced prosthetic head rather than degenerative joint space narrowing.

Therefore, the imaging findings are most consistent with dislocation of the left hemiarthroplasty.
caption: Postoperative anteroposterior pelvis radiograph demonstrating dislocation of bipolar hemiarthroplasty of the left hip
sub_caption: Postoperative anteroposterior pelvis radiograph demonstrating dislocation of bipolar hemiarthroplasty of the left hip
image_context: Forty days postoperatively, the patient was readmitted to our orthopaedic department complaining of acute left hip pain and inability to bear weight. No previous fall was reported. Radiographic evaluation revealed a dislocation of the left hip hemiarthroplasty (Figure <xref ref-type="fig" rid="cureus-0012-00000009044-i03">3</xref>).).
relevant_image_context: Forty days postoperatively, the patient was readmitted to our orthopaedic department complaining of acute left hip pain and inability to bear weight. No previous fall was reported. Radiographic evaluation revealed a dislocation of the left hip hemiarthroplasty (Figure 3).
